evals.name: refactor-draw-settings
evals.prompt: I'm working on dimgui (at /Users/isaiah/projects/dimgui) — a declarative ImGui wrapper using C++23 (cxxlatest). The src/main.cpp file has a function draw_settings that's pure imperative ImGui (eight widgets in one Begin/End block). Refactor it into a declarative form expressed through the dimgui header (include/dimgui/dimgui.hpp — currently a stub). Add only the widget specs you need. Build with `xmake f -m release -y && xmake build app` and confirm it compiles before stopping.
evals.expected_output: The agent identifies draw_settings as a refactor candidate, designs widget specs in include/dimgui/dimgui.hpp (Window/Text/Button/Slider*/Checkbox/InputText), updates main.cpp to use them, and verifies the build passes.

evals.name: design-wrapper-api
evals.prompt: I'm designing the public API for dimgui — a SwiftUI-style declarative wrapper around Dear ImGui — at /Users/isaiah/projects/dimgui/include/dimgui/dimgui.hpp. The header is currently a stub (just declares `version = 0`). Design and implement the API: Window, Text, Button, Checkbox, SliderFloat, SliderInt, InputText. Make it expressive enough to model what src/main.cpp's draw_settings does today, but DO NOT modify main.cpp — just build the API and confirm `xmake build app` still passes.
evals.expected_output: The agent designs widget spec types using the Type+State pattern (struct per widget kind, render() member), supports heterogeneous children via tuples or variadic templates, declares them in the header, and the existing main.cpp still builds.

evals.name: borderline-judgment
evals.prompt: Take a look at draw_debug_overlay in /Users/isaiah/projects/dimgui/src/main.cpp. Should it become declarative like draw_settings would, or is it fine as-is? If declarative, sketch what the call site would look like (no need to actually edit the file).
evals.expected_output: The agent considers the small size of draw_debug_overlay (2 lines of widgets), explicitly weighs Layer 1 vs Layer 2 vs leaving it imperative, and gives a reasoned recommendation rather than mechanically applying the pattern.

trigger_queries.query: Help me refactor draw_settings in dimgui's main.cpp from imperative ImGui calls into a declarative tree — the project is at /Users/isaiah/projects/dimgui and uses C++23 (cxxlatest)
I want to design a SwiftUI-style wrapper around imgui in C++23, can you help me think through the API for nested children?
this code is just a long list of ImGui::Begin/Text/Button/End calls — can we make it look more like the tree it's actually building?
I'm building a declarative C++ wrapper over wxWidgets and I'm stuck on how to model heterogeneous children that live in one expression
ok so I've got 30 lines of imperative ImGui setup in main.cpp, what's the cleanest C++23 way to express this as data and have one render function consume it?
show me how to use std::apply over a tuple of widget specs to render imgui in modern C++
this Begin/End block in main.cpp keeps growing every time someone adds a widget — what's the right pattern in modern C++ to keep the call site readable?
I want my ImGui code to read like Window{title, Text{...}, Button{...}}.render() but I don't know how to make children of different types live in one expression
the indentation in this Begin/End block already looks like a tree but the syntax is flat — annoying. is there a c++ pattern for that?
in dimgui (the wrapper i'm building over imgui), how do i make Window{} take a variadic pack of children of different types?
How do I link Dear ImGui with xmake on macOS? Getting linker errors about Cocoa and IOKit frameworks
My ImGui slider isn't updating the bound float value. I'm passing &value, what am I missing?
What's the difference between ImGui::SetNextWindowPos and SetNextWindowSize? Demo code uses both
I'm refactoring this Python data pipeline from imperative loops to a declarative pyspark DataFrame API — can you review my approach?
Help me set up a C++23 (cxxlatest) project with xmake — I'm getting 'imported module not found' errors
Can you explain how immediate-mode GUIs like ImGui differ from retained-mode like Qt? Trying to decide which to use for my tool
What's the best way to test ImGui code? Having trouble simulating clicks in unit tests
I have a std::vector<std::unique_ptr<Widget>> with virtual draw() — should I switch to std::variant<Widget...> for performance?
I want my UI to be data-driven — should I store widget definitions in JSON and load them at runtime?
show me the canonical Dear ImGui demo code so i can copy-paste a window with sliders and a button
